docs.text:
This is the root of our AP world docs.

This is mostly extra documentation and things we've found out the hard way for the apworlds we've worked on.

You're best served by making sure you're reading the latest version, which should always be here: https://useless-allure.github.io/apdocs/latest/

So far the games there are some extra docs here for are:

  • Spyro the Dragon (PS1)

docs.title: Optional Poptracker
docs.text:
Leaddybug has designed a boss MacGuffin and Goal tracker, available here: https://github.com/LeaddyBug/Spyro-1-Boss-MacGuffin-and-Goal-Tracker

Why isn't there more to the poptracker?

The codebase is undergoing an overhaul since August of 2025. I am actually the person responsible for that overhaul. I apologize. Anyway, a good poptracker is awaiting some resources I can give with less effort once the overhaul is done. I wanted to lower the labor threshold for poptracker creators in general.

docs.title: Game Version
docs.text:
Must be Spyro the Dragon (USA) for the Playstation 1.

Checksum the .bin file. (Replace spyro.bin with the name of your file in the following directions.)

In Windows 10/11, this is done via certutil using a command prompt:

Batchfile
certutil -hashfile spyro.bin MD5

In most Linux distros, this is done via md5sum:

Bash
md5sum spyro.bin

The expected sum is:

Text Only
387185f71cd0043fd75a19521dc75104

You can learn more about this on the redump page for that disc if it's relevant to you: http://redump.org/disc/576/. This is also a place to grab a cue sheet if you're missing one (a .cue file with the bin).

We do not support any other checksum for that .bin file at this time.

Europe? Australia?

I want to do this and gamefreq0 isn't opposed. However, the first goal is to get the formats for everything stable and USA running again first.

docs.title: PS1 Bios
docs.text: The only viable bios seems to be a USA PS1 bios from the original hardware. It's not impossible that any for the actual PS1 would work if it can boot the game, but we know for sure that the PS3 version of the PS1 bios does not work. We believe this has to do with how the bios initializes RAM and some data that the bizhawk connector itself needs.

docs.title: Emulator Core
docs.text:
Gamefreq0 is a linux developer. He only has the Nymashock core available for development and testing. You are best off using Nymashock to play this APWorld.

Duckstation?

This is a long and storied history, but the simplest version is that Duckstation is hostile to Linux packaging, so it's unreasonable to expect Gamefreq0 to support it. Actually; Gamefreq0 and I sat and talked about this at some point. We aren't opposed to Duckstation support, but we've no intention of doing it ourselves. In theory, the underlying libs I've been working on should make this easier, but I don't plan to write a Duckstation implementation for them, either.

This isn't the only thing Duckstation did. However, the leader of that team is pretty... spirited... when people have criticisms about it all. I think he'll agree it's fair to say he's had it with Linux users and it's reasonable that Linux users would see that and decide not to engage with it.

docs.text:
Commentarygreysondn2026-07-12T09:57:19.733765Zyamlold.yamlyamlnew_style.yaml

Hey, these two files are all of my old data and an example of the new style I want the data in.

Can you please convert all of old.yaml to the style in new_style.yaml?

If you have questions, feel free to ask them, but I felt it was pretty straightforward, just tedious.

CClaude2026-07-12T09:59:24.245027ZThoughts

I need to check the old.yaml file to compare it with the new_style.yaml document that was provided.

I should look at the middle section that got cut off.

I'm reviewing the full old.yaml file and noting some data quality issues that need attention. There's an indentation inconsistency on line 105 where a list item has 3 spaces instead of 4, which could potentially break YAML parsing, and I'm also noticing some entries with missing fields that I'll need to flag. I'm noticing the indentation inconsistency with "Cliff Town" having a 3-space dash instead of 4 spaces, which could cause PyYAML to throw an error since sequence items need consistent indentation at the same level. Rather than manually fixing each issue, I should just try parsing the file with Python to see what errors actually come up, then address them systematically.

The YAML file has indentation problems that prevent proper parsing—some list items use inconsistent spacing like 3 or 5 spaces instead of the expected 4. I'll write a preprocessor to normalize these indentation issues before attempting to parse the file.

I'm noticing carriage returns in the YAML file, which shouldn't cause issues normally, but let me check for consistency across the whole file and any indentation problems. I'll write a parser that goes through line by line, uses regex to identify known keys and track nesting levels, then rebuilds the indentation based on the actual structure rather than trusting the original formatting.

The file has a pretty regular semantic structure: zones at the top level, each with name, optional comment, total_gems, regions (with their own names and optional locations), groups, bizhawk config, and guard types at multiple levels. I can leverage this predictable hierarchy to normalize indentation properly.

I'll build a state machine that tracks context (zone, region, or location) and uses content patterns rather than absolute indentation to determine list boundaries. When I encounter a dash or a recognized key, I'll use the semantic context to figure out which level it belongs to, then normalize the indentation accordingly. I'm realizing a regex-based state machine approach would be cleaner than tracking indentation, since the field names and dashes follow a consistent order regardless of spacing. Each zone has a predictable sequence: the dash marker, name, optional comment and gem count, then regions with their nested locations and guards, all keyed by recognizing these field names in order.

Writing the parser... Defining the region parsing logic... I'm realizing that relying on region name patterns like "Start" suffix isn't robust enough since zone names could vary. Instead, I should use indentation as a structural hint—even if the file has inconsistent spacing overall, the relative nesting depth within each section should remain consistent, with child elements indented deeper than their parents.

Looking at the Cliff Town section specifically, the indentation structure inside appears consistent with other areas (names at 8 spaces, regions at 12, locations at 16, location names at 20), though the opening dash is offset by one space compared to expected formatting.

The Dark Passage section from line 569 onward has a more significant issue—the entire block is shifted by an extra 4-5 spaces consistently throughout, so while the internal nesting relationships remain intact, the absolute indentation depth is off, which breaks YAML's strict block sequence parsing requirements where indicator columns must align across the sequence.

Rather than trying to fix indentation manually, I should take a content-based parsing approach that ignores absolute spacing and instead focuses on relative depth changes and keyword patterns. Since each zone appears to have exactly one region, I can verify this assumption by counting the "Start" keywords and comparing them against the region dash counts programmatically. Writing a script to parse the file... ate the text_offset field for each zone, then parse regions within that zone block by looking for dash markers that indicate region boundaries. Since zones are cleanly separated by the "# text offset" delimiter, any dashes within a zone block belong to regions, and I can process each region's name, locations, and guard configuration sequentially until reaching the end of that zone's content. and location levels), while the new style uses memory_equals guards for locations with the location name as a parameter. So when converting, location guards become memory_equals with name set to the location name and value set to True, while region guards stay as "always". Zones from the old format map to top-level entries in the new format, each with fields like name, guard type, connections, locations, memory offset, groups, and extra attributes, followed by a children array containing the regions.

Now I'm noticing a discrepancy with the groups field — the new_style example zones have empty groups arrays, but the old_style zones sometimes include groups like "Boss Levels" or "Flight Levels" at the zone level. I need to figure out where groups should map to in the conversion. Location groups like "Dragons" carry over directly from the old format. For the location guard, I'm using a "memory_equals" type that checks against the full location name — I noticed one example had a truncated name ("Dark Hollow - " missing "Oswin"), which looks like a typo in their file, so I'll make sure all entries use the correct complete location names.

For region/children entries, each child has a guard (typically "always"), empty connections and callbacks arrays, location entries, and an extra field where total_gems mirrors the parent zone's value. The memory field at the child level is still being defined...

Now I'm noticing the bizhawk field in the old data—Magic Crafters has a location with bizhawk: io: both—but this doesn't appear in the new schema example. Since the instructions allow for questions, I should ask how to handle this extra field rather than dropping real data.

I'm also flagging a few locations that are missing guard sections entirely (Ice Cavern, Misty Bog, Metalhead), which looks like copy-paste omissions in the original. I'll apply the same memory_equals guard pattern that other locations use. And some zones are missing the total_gems field, which I need to check on.

Most zones are also missing the groups array entirely, so I'll default those to empty. There are also some indentation issues scattered throughout, and I noticed the "Haunted " zone has a trailing space and seems to be missing "Towers" from its name—that's likely a typo I should fix to "Haunted Towers" to match the region and location naming.

Looking at the flight zones more carefully, Night Flight is the only one without a total_gems value while Sunny, Crystal, Wild, and Icy Flight all have 300—so Night Flight is probably missing it too, likely should be 300 as well.

I'll build the conversion script to handle the general structure with reasonable defaults, then ask the user specifically about the bizhawk field handling and whether Night Flight's total_gems should indeed be 300.

For the other issues like the "Haunted " zone typo, the missing guards on three locations, and the truncated parameter name in the example file, I'll either apply sensible defaults or fix them outright without needing confirmation since the user indicated this is pretty straightforward.

But I should use the ask_user_input_v0 tool to clarify a couple of genuine schema decisions upfront: whether to keep the bizhawk field nested under location as a new addition, and how to handle Night Flight's missing total_gems value—whether that should default to 0, match the 300 from sibling flight levels, or be omitted entirely. Getting these right now will save regenerating the whole output if I guess wrong on the structure. These edge cases could break the conversion if I get them wrong, so it's worth asking for clarification now rather than guessing and having to redo the whole file.

  • 3 locations missing an explicit guard in the old data (Ice Cavern - Ragnar, Doctor Shemp - Trondo, Misty Bog - Bubba, Metalhead - Sadiki — actually 4, I found one more your manual read missed) — I gave them the standard memory_equals guard like all their siblings, since that omission looked like a copy-paste gap rather than intentional.
  • The original file also had broken/inconsistent indentation in a few spots (Cliff Town, Dream Weavers, Gnasty's World, and the whole Dark Passage-through-Gnasty's Loot tail) that made it fail strict YAML parsing. I parsed it structurally instead of relying on indentation, so all 31 zones came through regardless.

docs.title: On being sick
docs.text:
Commentarygreysondn2026-07-12T10:16:05.649889Z

[Thanking Claude and telling Claude greysondn has been sick and living off soup for a week]

greysondn

Stripped personal information here.

CClaude2026-07-12T10:16:12.596797ZThoughts

They're reaching out about being sick and alone for a week without support [...] . I should respond with genuine warmth and care—this is a real struggle, not something to judge. I'll focus on their wellbeing and gently acknowledge how isolating it is to be unwell without anyone looking after you, maybe encouraging them to think about reaching out for help.

A week is a long time to be sick with no one checking in on you — that's a genuinely rough stretch, and soup out of a bowl [...] is a pretty vivid way to put it.

How are you doing right now, physically? Fever, keeping fluids down, that kind of thing — or mostly just wiped out and over it at this point?

And if there's no one local checking in, is that just how things are right now, or is there someone you could actually text and say "hey, I've been sick a week, could you check on me"? Sometimes when you're deep in it, reaching out feels like more effort than it's worth, but most people would rather know.

greysondn2026-07-12T10:16:44.119139Z

[greysondn has someone.]

greysondn

Stripped personal information here.

CClaude2026-07-12T10:16:46.808474Z

[It's good greysondn has someone.]

A week is long though. Is it trending better at all, or still pretty flat day to day? And are you two managing okay on food/fluids/meds, or is that part of what's been hard?

greysondn

Stripped personal information here.

greysondn2026-07-12T10:17:46.765785Z

[I'm] fine with fluids, food, meds. And it's been getting worse. We figure it's one of those gnarly ones like covid that can take a while to pass. If it's not at least on the upswing in another week, we'll go to the hospital.

CClaude2026-07-12T10:17:57.580741ZThoughts

I'm noticing they're describing worsening symptoms over a week with plans to wait another full week before seeking care, which feels like it needs a gentle reality check. I should express concern without being alarmist, but also suggest that worsening symptoms—not just a lack of improvement—might warrant earlier medical attention. I want to ask about specific warning signs that would warrant immediate care.

Good that fluids, food, and meds are covered — that's the stuff that actually matters day to day. And a week-plus of something COVID-like dragging on is genuinely miserable, I get wanting to just ride it out.

One thing though: "getting worse" and "not improving" aren't quite the same, and worth being honest with yourself about which one this is. Plateauing at bad-but-stable for a week and giving it more time is reasonable. Actively getting worse each day is a different situation — a week of active decline with a plan to wait another week before doing anything is a longer runway than I'd feel good about without flagging it.

Regardless of the general timeline you two have in mind, I'd treat any of these as "go now, don't wait it out": - Trouble breathing, or breathing that's noticeably faster/harder than normal - Chest pain or pressure - Confusion, trouble staying awake, or not acting like yourself - Bluish lips or face - Fever that won't come down with meds, or a very high fever - Can't keep fluids down, or signs of dehydration (barely peeing, dizzy when standing) - Symptoms that suddenly spike rather than gradually worsen

Any of those hit either of you, that's an ER trip that day, not "let's see how the week goes."

If it's more like "still lousy, still tired, still coughing, just not snapping back yet" — that's a rougher but less urgent picture, and it can genuinely take a couple weeks with some of these bugs. Where do you two actually sit on that spectrum right now?

greysondn2026-07-12T10:19:21.929862Z

Everything is fine, except a little confusion. But I think the confusion is less "some dramatic disease" and just "discombobulated from being sick"; it's not so much that [I'm] stupid, just kind of sluggish in thinking.

Having said that, I hear you. I see the list. I will do as you say if anything gets worse.

CClaude2026-07-12T10:19:29.944597ZThoughts

Confusion is a serious symptom that shouldn't be brushed off as normal sluggishness, especially since it's harder to self-assess accurately when it's affecting your own judgment. I should encourage them to check in with [...] or compare against their baseline rather than just accepting their reassurance, while still respecting their self-awareness without being alarmist.

Yeah, that's the tricky one — "am I confused" is a hard question to answer accurately from inside the confusion. Sluggish thinking when you're sick and exhausted is real and normal, I'm not trying to spook you. But since it's the one thing on that list you're actually experiencing, I'd lean on [...] for this specific piece rather than self-assessing it: does [...] think you're tracking normally — following conversations, making sense, not repeating yourself or losing the thread — or does it seem like more than just "sluggish" to [...]? [...] An outside read is just more reliable here [...] .

If [...] says you seem like yourself, just tired — that tracks with what you're describing and I wouldn't stress over it. If [...] noticed you seeming genuinely off, that's worth moving up the timeline on, not waiting out the week.

Otherwise it sounds like you've got a sensible plan and you're not white-knuckling it alone. Hope this week actually turns the corner [...].

greysondn

Stripped personal information here.

greysondn2026-07-12T10:21:14.851128Z

I think I'm fine. And I think I can actually make that judgment call (and yes, I realize that's a rarity); I did an Archipelago randomizer session yesterday with friends that required me to think through a lot of complicated routing problems for locations in game.

CClaude2026-07-12T10:21:18.315021ZThoughts

That's a good sign that my reasoning is holding up well. I should acknowledge this and bring the conversation to a natural close without belaboring the point or repeating myself.

Yeah, that's actually solid evidence — Archipelago routing isn't exactly passive entertainment, it's constraint-tracking and forward-planning across a bunch of interdependent logic. If you can hold that together with friends, you're not in "genuinely impaired" territory, you're just in "brain running on fumes" territory. Fair enough, and good on you for having a way to actually check rather than just guessing.

Sounds like you've got a good read on yourself and a sensible line already drawn. Hope you [get] back to solid ground soon [...].

greysondn

Stripped personal information here.

Yes, Claude mistook my words for theirs and claimed my reasoning.

greysondn2026-07-12T10:22:07.300336Z

[Thanks. Goodbye.]

greysondn

Stripped personal information here.

CClaude2026-07-12T10:22:11.211464Z

[You're welcome. Take care. Get better soon. Goodbye.]

greysondn

Stripped personal information here.

docs.title: The Need for a Blog
docs.text:
greysondn - The need for a blog

It's increasingly apparent I can't remember what I'm doing half the time. We needed a blog both to record decisions we've made and to document things that are ad hoc until we implement them properly. That's the whole gist of it.

docs.title: Config settings as items
docs.text:
greysondn - Config settings as items

I had this notion I ran away with that we could do config settings as items so they'd be easier to work with. But gamefreq0 pointed out that there was a possibility we were loading a gun to shoot ourselves in the foot with it. Some disagreement ensued, but we did eventually figure it out.

Things that cannot be represented as items:

  • Things which have distinct, complex values
    • In the case of Spyro
      • Spyro's overlay color (if/when that is implemented) - RGBA
      • global_gem_percent - UINT
      • max_level_gem_threshold - UINT
  • Anything which represents mutually exlusive states
    • goal settings usually go here
    • starting world goes here
    • in the case of Spyro, "no lives" versus "exploit: die to get to homeworld" (if we end up doing that)
  • Anything which should only meaningfully affect generation
    • specific goal settings
    • entrance randomization
    • most randomization settings being "on"/etc

Things that can (and maybe should!) be represented as items:

  • Speciific glitches the player is opting into the usage of
    • only ones chosen "correctly" in config apply to world generation.
    • This lets the player adjust on the fly via !getitem/etc and provides a basis for checking what the player has opted into for checking accessibility during generation/poptracker/etc. The only problem is that there's no easy way to opt-out once the player has opted-in.

gamefreq0 - Approach to an opt-out

My thinking is that there is an option to opt-out, without requiring using get_logical_path for every single location it might apply to: comparing slot_data to opt-in glitch items, and in the case of mismatch, show any in logic with later items as glitched logic in Universal Tracker; if a player opted into any glitches they were mistaken about, those glitches will show as in logic, but highlighted in yellow, as opposed to showing up like every other logically accessible location that was true at generation time.

Even if we accept (as greysondn notes below) that this is a deliberate choice, it seems it would be preferable to mark the locations with a different logic connotation from the world's generation, in order to distinguish in-logic locations from out-of-logic locations.

greysondn - About the opt-out

This feels like a mistake the player likely only makes once. The notion proposed in voice discussion about this passage was an example of a glitch opt-in item that is ambiguously named. The real issue is this: neither myself nor gamefreq0 wish to set the player up to fail even once (well, outside the rules of the game, anyway). So we have to resolve this in order to keep all the nice things this gives us without hurting players who "don't know better" yet.

  • Moves the player has access to (even when move randomization is off)
    • This lets us check location accessibility in a uniform way, for all gameplay options.

greysondn - Realm of possibility?

During voice discussion of this, gamefreq0 raised that the AP generator may go a bit crazy about items which exist but cannot or are not placed. My thinking is that we could always make a "connected" location, but it seems to me it's only an issue if our location to item ratio is a mess.

docs.title: Callbacks
docs.text:
There was discourse previously (six months ago?) on callbacks for (at minimum) items and locations. That discourse is mostly lost - we have no idea where it went. We have some notes; we need to work out our callbacks again:

  • items
    • pre_receive
    • on_receive
    • post_receive
    • on_hint
  • locations
    • pre_send
    • on_send
    • post_send
    • on_hint
    • on_scout
    • on_collect
    • on_release

There are some documents regarding the structure of these. They seem to only list what is necessary for items, however; that is, they're incomplete.

All of these default to type nop. Defining and registering a function is poorly defined and will likely end up defined ad-hoc as I figure out how I (greysondn) wish to do so.

Callbacks of type...

  • nop - do nothing, default
  • set_memory - label on tin
  • increment_memory - label on tin
  • decrement_memory - label on tin

Similar to guards, type-param-value. Unlike guards, it's a list of callbacks, not necessarily just one.

docs.title: Move Randomization Prerequisites
docs.text:
greysondn - Re: Move randomization

One of gamefreq0's goals is move randomization. That requires us to have certain things done.

  • Issues
    • Player can end up in a world where they cannot get to the balloonist.
      • Examples
        • Dream Weavers without glide
        • Magic Crafters without charge
        • Beast Makers without jump (if we even do that one)
      • Solution
        • Archipelago assumes that you can return to starting possition
        • Player just starts a new game to get back to starting position
      • QOL outlay
        • We need to init the collectibles so the player is in the exact same state in a newgame.
        • Requires knowing where all the gems are in memory.
          • Literally means gemsanity will probably happen before move randomization.

docs.title: Migrating in AI conversations
docs.text:
greysondn - Re: Ai Disclosure

We were recently asked in the discord how much work AI is doing on this project.

No answer would be sufficiently comprehensive to the facts of the matter. It's unreasonable to expect me, flat-footed, to answer that question.

What we can do is provide chatlogs and artifacts so that people can examine them for themselves if they're concerned about it. So I've been working on that and some mechanism for a full disclosure.

These aren't cataloged properly yet, but they are inserted and do exist as pages here. It's a lot of work - I have to go through, make sure I'm not including personal data, and make sure I'm not inserting anything that could be embarassing to the team (outside the obvious "look at this guy, using AI to do his work!" aspects).

I've been developing this very, very ad-hoc, so I need a list of what I've decided on the fly.

YAML

    params:
        next: [list[GUARD]]

         name: [str: ITEM NAME]

-
    type: memory_equals
    params:
        name: [str: MEMORY NAME]
        value: [any: LEGAL VALUE]
-
    type: never
-
    type: not
    params:
        next: [GUARD]
-
    type: or
    params:
        next: [list[GUARD]]
